null_verdict: same hard words corrected in both, but DIFFERENT errors (convergence + editorial coupling, NOT copying — see direct test)
finding: DIRECT test (Ahlborn, decisive): MW shares the PWG headword error in only 2/123 (1.6%) cases — where PWG erred, MW has the CORRECT form (90) or lacks the word (31). MW did NOT inherit PWG's headword mistakes. NULL test: headwords corrected in BOTH = 256 vs 102.8 expected (lift 2.49, p=4.01e-41) — the SAME hard words are error-prone in both, but with DIFFERENT errors. That lift is convergence on difficult vocabulary PLUS editorial coupling (cross-dict fix-bundles like pwgissues correct the same word in several dicts by design), NOT copied errors — the direct test rules copying out. Conclusion: MW inherited Böhtlingk's APPARATUS/content (F1 citations, F2 homonyms), not his mechanical errors; the books were independently typeset. (Many ahlborn errors are err='scan' = digitization artifacts a separately-keyed MW could not share anyway.)
